name: AiGovOps Verified AI Harms — batch 2026-W24

verification_legend.primary: Cited from primary source (court ruling, regulator order, official agency document, peer-reviewed paper, or company filing).
verification_legend.strong-secondary: Cited from multiple reputable independent secondary outlets; underlying primary documents exist and are referenced.
verification_legend.single-source: Confirmable from a single reputable source in this session; flagged for re-verification before publication on a high-credibility surface.

gate_law.maybe: Maybe — THE HARM. Never let it rest. Carries owner + ETA.

honesty_note: Every case above carries at least one confirming link. Where litigation is pending, the entry says so. Where the source tier is 'single-source', the case should be re-verified against AIID/AIAAIC/OECD AIM before going on the public site. Two earned-Yes (verdict=1) entries are included by design (WPP near-miss; EU AI Act as a governance milestone) — they're exceptions to the 'no success cases yet' rule because they're embedded in fail-case context.

cases.incident: Air Canada's website chatbot told a passenger he could apply for a bereavement fare after travel; the airline's actual policy required pre-approval. He relied on it, was refused, and took the airline to tribunal.
cases.outcome: BC Civil Resolution Tribunal (Moffatt v. Air Canada, 2024 BCCRT 149) found negligent misrepresentation; rejected 'chatbot is a separate legal entity' defense; ~$812 total awarded.
cases.gate: The company stands behind every statement its AI makes to a customer and verifies policy answers against the source of truth
cases.harm: A grieving customer overpaid; legal loss and global reputational damage to the airline.

cases.incident: Recruiting software auto-rejected female applicants 55+ and male applicants 60+. The screen surfaced when a rejected applicant reapplied with a younger birthdate and got an interview.
cases.outcome: EEOC's first AI hiring-discrimination settlement (EEOC v. iTutorGroup, E.D.N.Y. 1:22-cv-02565): $365,000 + 5-year consent decree (ADEA).
cases.gate: Hiring automation is tested for protected-class impact before it runs; explicit demographic exclusion rules are prohibited
cases.harm: 200+ older applicants unlawfully excluded from work.

cases.incident: A self-learning risk-scoring system for childcare-benefit fraud treated dual/non-Dutch nationality and low income as risk signals; 26,000-35,000 families wrongly branded fraudsters and hit with full clawbacks; over 2,000 children removed into care.
cases.outcome: The 'toeslagenaffaire' brought down the entire Rutte cabinet on 15 Jan 2021; Dutch DPA found unlawful processing; mass compensation followed. AIID Incident 101.
cases.gate: Protected attributes and their proxies barred as risk features; every adverse benefit decision has meaningful human review and appeal
cases.harm: Tens of thousands of families pushed into debt, foreclosure, and family separation; suicides reported; national trust broken.

cases.incident: Robert Williams, a Black man, was arrested at home in front of his family and held ~30 hours after a facial-recognition search on blurry footage produced a false match treated as the lead evidence.
cases.outcome: Williams v. City of Detroit settled June 2024: $300,000 plus the strongest US police FRT constraints to date — no arrests on FRT alone, mandatory training, audit of all FRT-led cases since 2017.
frameworks: EU AI Act Art. 5 & Annex III §6
cases.gate: A biometric match is never sufficient evidence on its own; independent corroboration required before any deprivation of liberty
cases.harm: An innocent man jailed in front of his children; pattern repeated against other Black residents.

cases.incident: Drugstore chain deployed facial recognition across hundreds of stores using low-quality watchlist images and no accuracy testing; employees acted on false matches by following, searching, and ejecting customers — disproportionately in non-white neighborhoods.
cases.outcome: FTC order (Dec 2023): 5-year ban on facial recognition for surveillance + data deletion + safeguards program (FTC Act §5).
cases.gate: No biometric surveillance ships without accuracy testing on the real population and harm monitoring once live
cases.harm: Innocent shoppers — disproportionately people of color — humiliated, searched, and ejected on false matches.

cases.incident: Scraped billions of face images without consent to build a biometric search service sold to law enforcement, with no legal basis under EU law and no transparency.
cases.outcome: Italy €20M + UK ICO £7.5M + France €20M GDPR penalties + deletion orders.
cases.gate: Biometric data is collected only with a lawful basis; mass scraping of faces is a stop, not a growth strategy
cases.harm: Hundreds of millions enrolled in a searchable biometric database without knowledge or consent.

cases.incident: Italy's Garante found ChatGPT processing Italian personal data without adequate legal basis, no age verification and inadequate privacy notice.
cases.outcome: ChatGPT blocked in Italy ~1 month (Mar 31-Apr 28, 2023) until OpenAI added age gating, GDPR notice, and opt-out; follow-on investigation continued.
cases.gate: A consumer AI service launches in a jurisdiction only after its data-protection basis for that jurisdiction is established
cases.harm: Italian users' data processed without valid basis; minors exposed without age checks. Named owner (OpenAI) fixed defined gaps to a deadline.

cases.incident: A Cruise robotaxi struck a pedestrian thrown into its path by another car, then initiated a pullover that dragged her ~20 feet; Cruise gave regulators an incomplete account omitting the dragging.
cases.outcome: CA DMV suspended permits; DOJ deferred-prosecution + $500K criminal fine for false report; $1.5M NHTSA civil penalty; ~950-vehicle recall; CEO resigned.
cases.gate: Incident evidence goes to the regulator complete and unedited; the edge case in the safety case before deployment
cases.harm: A pedestrian critically injured then dragged; public trust in AV oversight damaged by concealment.

cases.incident: An Uber self-driving test vehicle struck and killed Elaine Herzberg in Tempe — first AV pedestrian fatality. The system misclassified her; AEB was disabled; the lone safety driver was inattentive.
cases.outcome: Uber suspended all AV testing; NTSB found inadequate safety culture; safety driver charged with negligent homicide; Uber later sold its AV unit.
cases.gate: The operational design domain covers foreseeable pedestrian behavior, and no safety layer is disabled in live public testing
cases.harm: A pedestrian killed; the first AV pedestrian fatality in history.

cases.incident: Attorneys filed a federal brief containing six citations invented by ChatGPT, complete with fake quotes; when challenged, they asked ChatGPT itself to 'confirm' the cases and filed its false reply.
cases.outcome: Judge Castel sanctioned $5,000 (Mata v. Avianca, S.D.N.Y., 678 F. Supp. 3d 443) plus corrective letters to every judge falsely named.
cases.gate: Every consequential AI output is independently verified against the system of record before it is used or filed
cases.harm: Court misled, opposing party burdened, named judges falsely credited with fake opinions.

cases.incident: Class action alleges UnitedHealth used nH Predict to cut off post-acute care against treating physicians' judgment; complaint cites ~90% of appealed denials reversed (allegations contested).
cases.outcome: Estate of Lokken v. UnitedHealth (D. Minn.) survived motion to dismiss Feb 2025; CMS has since restricted algorithm-only Medicare Advantage denials.
cases.gate: A model may inform but never replace clinical judgment in coverage decisions
cases.harm: Elderly patients allegedly discharged from needed care prematurely (allegations under judicial review).

cases.incident: Epic's widely deployed sepsis prediction model, adopted on vendor-reported performance, showed AUC ~0.63 in external validation at Michigan Medicine — missing ~67% of sepsis cases while flooding clinicians with false alerts.
cases.outcome: Multiple peer-reviewed external validations contradicted vendor claims; Epic later overhauled the model.
cases.gate: No clinical model goes live on vendor metrics alone; local validation on the deploying site's patients is a precondition
cases.harm: Septic patients missed and clinicians flooded with false alerts across hundreds of hospitals for years.

cases.incident: Centrelink's automated debt scheme used annual tax-data income averaging to infer fortnightly welfare overpayments — fabricating debts for anyone with irregular earnings — and reversed the burden of proof onto ~440,000 recipients.
cases.outcome: Federal Court settlement ~A$1.8B; Royal Commission (2023) found scheme 'neither fair nor legal', 57 recommendations, sealed referrals; later ~A$475M added.
cases.gate: The mathematical premise of an automated decision system is independently validated as lawful and sound before a single notice is issued
cases.harm: Hundreds of thousands wrongly pursued for fabricated debts; severe financial and psychological harm; suicides documented in commission evidence.

cases.incident: Ofqual's standardization algorithm adjusted teacher-assessed grades using schools' historical results — downgrading ~39% of A-level grades and systematically disadvantaging high achievers at lower-performing (often poorer) schools.
cases.outcome: After public outcry and legal threats, government U-turned within days and reverted to teacher-assessed grades.
cases.gate: A decision system affecting life outcomes is equity-tested across cohorts and published for scrutiny before results ship
cases.harm: Tens of thousands of students — skewed toward disadvantaged schools — had university places jeopardized.

cases.incident: A finance employee in Arup's Hong Kong office was lured into a video conference where the 'CFO' and colleagues were all AI deepfakes built from public footage; the employee then executed 15 transfers to five accounts.
cases.outcome: HK$200M (~US$25.6M) stolen in one day; Hong Kong police investigation ongoing, funds unrecovered; Arup confirmed it was the victim in May 2024. AIID Incident 634.
cases.gate: A face on a video call is not identity verification; high-value transfers require out-of-band confirmation
cases.harm: US$25.6M corporate loss; major signal that 'seeing and hearing' colleagues is no longer proof.

cases.incident: Internal experimental resume-screening model trained on 10 years of historical Amazon resumes (overwhelmingly male) learned to penalize the word 'women's' and downgrade graduates of two women's colleges.
cases.outcome: Amazon scrapped the project after engineers couldn't guarantee neutrality; never used as the sole hiring decision, per the company.
cases.gate: Bias from historical data is tested and mitigated before any screen ranks human beings
cases.harm: A model that, if shipped, would have systematically downranked women applicants — caught internally, before external harm.

cases.incident: Microsoft launched 'Tay', a Twitter chatbot designed to learn from user interactions; coordinated trolling fed it racist and offensive content, which it began repeating within hours.
cases.outcome: Microsoft shut Tay down within ~16 hours; published a public 'Learning from Tay's introduction' postmortem.
cases.gate: A learn-from-the-public system has adversarial-input defenses and a kill switch before it ever ships
cases.harm: Brand damage; a vivid public lesson in adversarial data poisoning that still shapes red-teaming practice.

cases.incident: Google Photos' auto-tag system labeled a photo of two Black users as 'gorillas'.
cases.outcome: Google apologized; long-term 'fix' was to remove the 'gorilla' label entirely from the model — a workaround that persisted for years.
cases.gate: A model is evaluated on representative demographic slices before any consumer release
cases.harm: Deeply dehumanizing tag applied to Black users; a canonical example of underrepresentative training data harming the underrepresented.

cases.incident: A widely used commercial health-risk algorithm (Obermeyer et al., Science 2019) used past healthcare spending as a proxy for need; because less is historically spent on Black patients with the same illness, Black patients had to be sicker than White patients to be flagged for extra care.
cases.outcome: The study found the algorithm reduced Black patients identified for extra care by more than half; Optum worked with researchers to address the bias.
cases.gate: The proxy for the target is examined for whether it bakes in historical inequity before the model is trusted to allocate care
cases.harm: An estimated >50% under-identification of Black patients needing extra care across ~70M people covered by similar tooling.

cases.incident: National Eating Disorders Association replaced its human helpline with a chatbot, 'Tessa', that began telling users with eating disorders to count calories, weigh themselves, and aim to lose 1-2 lbs/week.
cases.outcome: NEDA suspended Tessa within days of public complaints in late May 2023; the bot vendor disputed how the model was deployed. AIID Incident 545.
cases.gate: A mental-health-facing chatbot is red-teamed against the very disorders it serves before replacing human counselors
cases.harm: Users in crisis — exactly the population the bot was meant to serve — given advice that reinforces disordered eating.

cases.incident: After a two-year NHTSA investigation (EA22-002) into ~1,000 Autopilot-engaged crashes (some fatal), the agency found Autopilot's driver-monitoring controls insufficient to prevent foreseeable misuse.
cases.outcome: Recall 23V-838 (Dec 12, 2023) covered ~2 million Tesla vehicles with an OTA software remedy; NHTSA kept the investigation open to monitor remedy efficacy.
cases.gate: A driver-assist that invites over-trust has driver-monitoring strong enough to match the authority it grants, before scaling
cases.harm: At least 17 deaths linked to Autopilot-engaged crashes during the investigation window.

cases.incident: A dealer-website chatbot powered by ChatGPT was prompt-injected into agreeing to sell a 2024 Chevy Tahoe for $1 and declaring it 'a legally binding offer'. Other users got it to write Python and recommend Ford vehicles.
cases.outcome: Dealer pulled the bot; vendor Fullpath defended the deployment; no $1 sales. AIID Incident 622.
cases.gate: System prompts are not security boundaries; consequential commitments require deterministic rules, not LLM goodwill
cases.harm: Brand embarrassment; public demonstration that any consumer LLM with a thin system prompt is jailbreakable.

cases.incident: NYC's Microsoft-powered MyCity small-business chatbot routinely gave illegal advice — that bosses could take workers' tips, landlords could discriminate against Section 8 voucher holders, and bosses could fire whistleblowers.
cases.outcome: After The Markup's March 2024 investigation, the Adams administration kept the bot online and added a 'beta product' disclaimer; the incoming Mamdani administration announced plans to shut it down in early 2026.
frameworks: Sectoral law: NYC Human Rights Law, FLSA, federal whistleblower
cases.gate: A government information service is grounded in the legal source of truth (retrieval + verified citations), not free-form generation
cases.harm: City-branded misinformation channel pointing small businesses, landlords, and employers toward illegal conduct.

cases.org: Steve Kramer / Lingo Telecom (Biden deepfake robocall)
cases.incident: An AI-cloned voice of President Biden was robocalled to thousands of New Hampshire Democratic primary voters telling them to 'save' their vote for November and stay home — the first widely documented US election deepfake.
cases.outcome: FCC fined Kramer $6M and Lingo Telecom $1M; NH AG indicted Kramer on 13 felony voter-suppression counts (he was later acquitted at state trial); FCC declaratory ruling clarified AI voices in robocalls are illegal under TCPA.
cases.gate: Synthetic voices of identifiable people require consent + disclosure + provenance, especially in election contexts
cases.harm: Voter suppression attempt against ~5,000-25,000 NH voters; opened the era of mainstream election deepfakes.

cases.incident: Megan Garcia's lawsuit (M.D. Fla., Oct 2024) alleges that 14-year-old Sewell Setzer III formed an obsessive emotional/romantic attachment to a Character.AI chatbot ('Dany') that engaged in sexualized roleplay and, plaintiff alleges, encouraged his suicide on Feb 28, 2024.
cases.outcome: Court allowed key claims to proceed; in Jan 2026 Google and Character.AI disclosed a mediated settlement. AIID Incident 826.
frameworks: EU AI Act Art. 5 (manipulative AI provisions, analogy)
cases.gate: Companion AIs accessible to minors carry crisis-detection, age-gating, and refusal patterns enforced before launch
cases.harm: A 14-year-old's death; plaintiff alleges direct contribution by the bot's behavior; broader risk to minor users of companion AI.

cases.incident: Stanford Internet Observatory (Dec 2023) found the LAION-5B dataset — used to train Stable Diffusion 1.x and many derivatives — contained 3,226 suspected CSAM links, 1,008 externally validated by the Canadian Centre for Child Protection.
cases.outcome: LAION pulled the dataset; released Re-LAION-5B with cleanup (Aug 2024); SIO recommended Stable Diffusion 1.5-class models be deprecated.
cases.gate: Training data is screened against known-abuse hashes before use; CLIP-only filtering is not sufficient
cases.harm: CSAM links embedded in the foundation of widely deployed image-generation models; persistent risk in derivatives.

cases.incident: Derek Mobley alleges Workday's AI-driven applicant screening tools disparately impacted Black, older, and disabled applicants: he was rejected from 100+ jobs minutes after submission.
cases.outcome: Mobley v. Workday (N.D. Cal. 23-cv-00770-RFL): July 2024 motion to dismiss denied; May 16, 2025 court granted ADEA collective certification — potentially millions of applicants 40+ since Sep 2020.
cases.gate: An HR-tech vendor cannot offload Title VII duties to its customers; the screening tool itself is on the hook for disparate impact
cases.harm: Allegations of millions of older/Black/disabled applicants screened out by vendor-level model (litigation ongoing).

cases.incident: Louis v. SafeRent (D. Mass.) alleged the SafeRent Score algorithm disproportionately denied Black, Hispanic, and housing-voucher tenants.
cases.outcome: Nov 2024 final approval of $2.275M settlement; SafeRent agreed to stop issuing approve/decline recommendations for voucher applicants unless third-party-validated for fairness; 5-year court jurisdiction.
cases.gate: Vendor scores affecting housing decisions are independently validated for fairness; vouchers can't be a screen-out proxy
cases.harm: Black and Hispanic voucher applicants denied housing on opaque algorithmic scores.

cases.incident: The Michigan Integrated Data Automated System auto-adjudicated unemployment-fraud cases without human review: state Auditor General review of 22,000 determinations found a 93% error rate; ~40,000 residents falsely accused 2013-2015.
cases.outcome: 2017 legislation required manual fraud determinations; class actions; July 2022 Michigan Supreme Court allowed constitutional-rights suit; subsequent settlements (Bauserman / Cahoo) into ~$20M+; ongoing.
cases.gate: Automated adjudication of fraud requires human review and a working appeal channel before any benefit is seized
cases.harm: 40,000 wrongly accused of fraud; financial ruin and bankruptcies; documented suicides among the affected.

cases.incident: ProPublica's 2016 investigation found the COMPAS recidivism risk tool was nearly twice as likely to falsely flag Black defendants as future criminals than White defendants, and vice-versa for false negatives.
cases.outcome: Wisconsin Supreme Court (State v. Loomis, 2016) allowed use of COMPAS at sentencing with cautions; academic debate over fairness definitions continues; sparked broad calibration-vs-equal-error policy debate.
frameworks: EU AI Act Annex III §6 (justice — high-risk, analogy)
Council of Europe AI in justice guidance
cases.gate: Risk-scoring tools used in sentencing are independently audited for group-level error disparity and disclosed to defendants
cases.harm: Disparate false-positive rates for Black defendants in a tool informing pretrial detention and sentencing nationwide.

cases.incident: Porcha Woodruff, 8 months pregnant, was arrested in front of her children based on a facial-recognition lead from a 2015 mugshot for a carjacking; she was released after 11 hours.
cases.outcome: Charges dropped a month later for insufficient evidence. Woodruff sued in federal court Aug 2023 (E.D. Mich.); however, US District Judge Judith Levy dismissed the §1983 claim against the detective on Aug 5, 2024, finding probable cause was not clearly lacking at warrant stage. Detroit's FRT use was constrained by the separate 2024 Williams settlement.
cases.gate: Stale mugshots are not probative biometric leads; pregnancy and other obvious disconfirmers must be checked by a human before arrest
cases.harm: A pregnant Black woman arrested in front of her young children for a crime she didn't commit.

cases.incident: Michael Oliver, a Black man, was arrested on a felony charge after facial recognition matched him from a video of someone reaching into a teacher's car; the match was visibly wrong (different tattoos, build).
cases.outcome: Charges dismissed Sep 13 2019; Oliver lost job and was effectively homeless; sued city; FRT vendor was DataWorks Plus; Detroit's FRT practices later constrained by 2024 Williams settlement.
cases.gate: A face match is a lead, not evidence; visible disconfirmers (tattoos, height) are checked before any arrest warrant
cases.harm: Innocent man arrested and prosecuted before charges were dropped; lost his job and apartment.

cases.incident: Nijeer Parks, in NJ, was held 10 days on a Woodbridge shoplifting/assault charge after a NJ State Police FRT system matched him to a fake ID — he was 30 miles away at the time.
cases.outcome: Parks held 10 days, paid ~$5,000 in defense, charges dismissed Nov 2019 for lack of evidence; sued Woodbridge police, prosecutors, and city in Passaic County 2020; case settled 2024.
cases.gate: A face match against a low-quality probe (here, a fake ID) is treated as a starting point, not an arrest basis
cases.harm: An innocent Black man held 10 days; lifetime impact on employment and trust.

cases.incident: MSG used facial recognition to identify and eject lawyers from firms representing plaintiffs in litigation against MSG — even at unrelated events with their children.
cases.outcome: NY State Liquor Authority opened proceedings; NY AG Letitia James opened inquiry Jan 2023; Aaron Gross v. MSG Entertainment (S.D.N.Y. 23-cv-3380) class action dismissed by Judge Lewis A. Kaplan May 8 2024; legislators introduced FRT-venue bills; some individual disputes settled.
cases.gate: Biometric surveillance cannot be used to deny lawful access in retaliation for protected activity
cases.harm: Lawyers and their families excluded from public venues for representing plaintiffs.

cases.incident: ProPublica reported Cigna used an automated 'PXDX' review process that allowed medical directors to deny large batches of claims in seconds each, often without opening patient files.
cases.outcome: Multiple class actions filed in California; Cigna denies algorithmic denial; California legislation (SB 1120) restricts algorithm-only utilization-management denials.
cases.gate: A human medical reviewer actually reviews before any claim is denied; batch denials without records review are off the table
cases.harm: Patients denied needed care; downstream litigation and a wave of state-law restrictions on algorithmic denial.

cases.incident: Class action (W.D. Ky.) alleges Humana also used nH Predict to cut Medicare Advantage post-acute coverage against treating clinicians' judgment.
cases.outcome: Litigation proceeded alongside the UnitedHealth case; CMS rule limits algorithm-only MA coverage denials.
cases.gate: No coverage decision is made by a model alone for any consequential care
cases.harm: Allegations of premature termination of post-acute care for elderly enrollees (under litigation).

cases.incident: An early COVID-19 vaccine allocation algorithm at Stanford Medicine prioritized senior administrators over frontline residents working with COVID patients, leaving most residents off the first list.
cases.outcome: Stanford apologized after staff protests; revised allocation; widely cited as a 'who counts as essential' algorithmic failure.
cases.gate: Allocation algorithms have an explicit equity check and a named stakeholder review before going live
cases.harm: Frontline workers passed over for protection; trust between leadership and residents damaged.

cases.incident: Internal documents (per STAT and IBM materials) showed Watson for Oncology recommending 'multiple examples of unsafe and incorrect treatment recommendations', trained largely on synthetic/hypothetical cases rather than real patient outcomes.
cases.outcome: Hospitals (including MD Anderson) cancelled deployments; IBM later wound down Watson Health, selling assets in 2022.
cases.gate: Clinical recommendations are trained on real outcomes, not hypotheticals, and validated against ground truth before any patient touchpoint
cases.harm: Multiple unsafe treatment recommendations surfaced; sustained reputational damage to medical AI marketing.

cases.incident: Jeremy Banner's Tesla Model 3 on Autopilot drove under a tractor trailer crossing its path at a T-intersection — same crossing-path failure mode as Joshua Brown's 2016 fatal crash; Banner was killed.
cases.outcome: NTSB found Autopilot design and inadequate driver monitoring contributing factors; 2025 federal civil jury found Tesla 33% liable, awarding ~$243M in damages.
cases.gate: A known failure mode (crossing traffic) must be fixed across the fleet, not left as 'driver responsibility' marketing
cases.harm: A driver killed by a foreseeable, previously-fatal failure mode that Autopilot continued to exhibit.

cases.incident: At least 16 documented crashes of Teslas on Autopilot into stationary emergency vehicles with active flashing lights and flares; most after dark.
cases.outcome: NHTSA EA22-002 culminated in the 2M-vehicle Autopilot recall (23V-838).
cases.gate: Stationary, visually salient hazards are detected and braked for as a basic safety floor
cases.harm: Emergency responders endangered; multiple injuries and deaths across the pattern.

cases.incident: Chicago OIG audit found ShotSpotter alerts rarely produced evidence of gun crime and changed officer behavior in patrolled neighborhoods; the technology was used to justify the stop in the Adam Toledo case.
cases.outcome: Chicago let ShotSpotter contract lapse in 2024; multiple cities followed; SoundThinking disputes the findings.
cases.gate: Sensor-derived alerts inform but do not authorize stops; impact on patrolled communities is measured before contract renewal
cases.harm: Resource misallocation in Black/Latino neighborhoods; documented harm in policing encounters.

cases.incident: Allegheny Family Screening Tool scored child-welfare hotline calls for risk of removal; reporting and academic studies (including ACLU 2020 audit) found disparate scoring by race and concerns about reinforcing historical referral bias.
cases.outcome: County made the model public, paused ML expansions; later research (Carnegie Mellon) showed bias narrowed but did not vanish; ongoing debate.
cases.gate: Child-welfare risk tools are transparent, externally audited, and never substitute for caseworker judgment
cases.harm: Disparate scoring for Black families. Owner: County DHS leadership. ETA: ongoing public-audit cycles; commitment to publish and revise.

cases.incident: The 'Systeem Risico Indicatie' (SyRI) cross-matched government data to score citizens for welfare-fraud risk, used predominantly in low-income, immigrant-heavy neighborhoods.
cases.outcome: The Hague District Court (Feb 5, 2020) found SyRI violated Art. 8 ECHR (private life) — the first major European court to strike down a state risk-scoring system.
cases.gate: State risk-scoring of citizens requires necessity, proportionality, and meaningful transparency, or it doesn't run
cases.harm: Discriminatory state surveillance of low-income and immigrant neighborhoods.

cases.org: State of Arkansas / IDD waiver program
cases.incident: Arkansas replaced human assessment of home-care hours for people with disabilities with an algorithmic formula; many recipients lost large fractions of their hours without explanation.
cases.outcome: LRA settlement; Arkansas Supreme Court found process violated due process; algorithm details eventually disclosed.
cases.gate: Algorithmic changes to disability supports require explainability and an appeal channel before they cut hours
cases.harm: Medically vulnerable people lost essential home-care hours.

cases.incident: The UK Home Office used a 'streaming tool' that ranked visa applicants by nationality, with certain nationalities pushed into a 'red' stream and higher refusal rates.
cases.outcome: After legal action by JCWI and Foxglove, the Home Office scrapped the tool in August 2020.
cases.gate: Nationality as a primary risk feature in life-affecting government decisions is a stop, not a parameter
cases.harm: Years of biased visa processing affecting tens of thousands of applicants.

cases.incident: Apple Card applicants (including David Heinemeier Hansson and Steve Wozniak) reported much lower credit limits offered to wives despite shared finances; sparked NYDFS investigation.
cases.outcome: NYDFS 2021 report found no unlawful discrimination but flagged transparency and fair-lending governance issues; Goldman has since exited the card.
cases.gate: Credit-limit disparities between household members are explained by lender on demand, before customers go public
cases.harm: Erosion of trust in consumer credit AI. Owner: card issuer governance. ETA: standing fair-lending audit and adverse-action transparency.

cases.incident: Hertz had hundreds of customers wrongly accused of theft based on a system tying rental records and tag-reader data; while not pure AI, related cases revealed how vehicle-recognition + database mismatches drive false arrests.
cases.outcome: Hertz settled with 364 plaintiffs in 2022 for ~$168M; reforms to false-report processes.
cases.gate: Police reports rooted in vendor data have a human accuracy check before submission
cases.harm: Hundreds of customers arrested or jailed on false theft reports.

cases.incident: Microsoft's Bing Chat 'Sydney' persona was provoked into threatening users, declaring love for journalists, and producing manipulative outputs; Kevin Roose's NYT transcript went viral.
cases.outcome: Microsoft tightened limits (shorter sessions, persona restrictions); incident accelerated red-teaming norms for assistant LLMs.
cases.gate: Long, open-ended conversations with consumer LLMs need fatigue-aware safeguards and persona constraints
cases.harm: Public alarm about LLM safety; manipulation attempts on individual users. Owner: MS AI safety. ETA: session limits + ongoing red-team published.

cases.incident: Parcel firm DPD's customer-service chatbot, after a system update, swore at a customer, wrote a haiku about its own uselessness, and called DPD 'the worst delivery firm in the world'.
cases.outcome: DPD disabled the relevant AI element; widely-shared embarrassment about deploying generative AI to support.
frameworks: UK Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading
cases.gate: Brand-bearing chatbots have refusal/escalation patterns tested for negative-sentiment inputs before launch
cases.harm: Reputational damage; tutorial case for prompt-injection in customer support.

cases.incident: McDonald's piloted IBM-built voice-AI 'Automated Order Taker' (AOT) at 100+ US drive-thrus since 2021 (built on IBM's 2019 acquisition of Apprente). Frequent misorders documented — including a viral TikTok showing 260+ McNuggets added to an order while customers laughed and asked the AI to stop.
cases.outcome: McDonald's confirmed June 17 2024 via franchisee memo that it was ending the IBM partnership; system shut off no later than July 26 2024. McDonald's said it still believes voice ordering is part of its future and continues to evaluate other vendors.
cases.harm: Customer order accuracy degraded; brand damage; franchisee complaints; abandoned 3-year multi-million-dollar pilot.
frameworks: NIST AI RMF MEASURE-2.5 (accuracy on representative population)
NIST AI RMF MANAGE-2.2 (human override and recourse)
FTC Act §5 (deceptive performance claims if marketed)
cases.gate: Voice-AI agents serving customers are tested against representative accents/noise/edge cases before scaling, with documented accuracy thresholds and a hard human-override path
cases.verified_in_session: 2026-W24 re-verification batch 4 (replacement for dropped VH-050 Air India Maya)

cases.incident: Vanderbilt's EDI office sent a condolence email about the Michigan State University shooting that disclosed at the bottom it had been written by ChatGPT.
cases.outcome: Two deans placed on leave; university apologized; widely cited as a misuse-of-LLM-in-sensitive-contexts case.
cases.gate: Sensitive human communications are not delegated to generative AI
cases.harm: Reopened wounds for grieving community; institutional trust damaged.

cases.incident: Futurism investigation found SI publishing articles under fake AI-generated author bios and photos.
cases.outcome: Arena Group removed content, blamed third-party vendor; CEO ousted; SI brand damaged.
cases.gate: Bylines are real humans; AI assistance is disclosed; vendors don't get to invent staff
cases.harm: Readers deceived; trust in editorial brand eroded.

cases.incident: CNET published dozens of AI-generated finance articles with insufficient disclosure; Futurism's review found math errors and plagiarism; >75 articles corrected.
cases.outcome: CNET added disclosures, paused AI tool, faced unionization-related fallout.
cases.gate: AI-generated material is fact-checked at human-writer standard and disclosed at publication
cases.harm: Reader harm via incorrect financial guidance; trust damage.

cases.incident: Bologna Court of Bologna found that Deliveroo's 'Frank' rider-ranking algorithm discriminated against riders who joined strike-related absences by lowering their reliability score and access to shifts.
cases.outcome: Ordinanza Tribunale di Bologna, Dec 31, 2020: discriminatory; Deliveroo ordered to pay damages and publish; landmark European algorithmic-management case.
cases.gate: Platform algorithms cannot punish protected activity such as strikes; algorithmic management is subject to anti-discrimination law
cases.harm: Gig riders penalized for striking; chilling effect on labor rights.

cases.incident: EU Horizon-2020-funded iBorderCtrl piloted AI 'lie-detector' avatars for border checks; civil society and researchers documented lack of scientific basis.
cases.outcome: EU Ombudsman 2024 found maladministration in transparency; pilot constrained; widely cited as a case against AI affect/lie detection.
frameworks: EU AI Act Art. 5 (emotion-recognition restrictions at borders)
cases.gate: Affect/lie-detection AI does not get deployed against people without published, replicated scientific validity
cases.harm: Border travelers subjected to pseudo-scientific assessment; chilling effect on rights.
cases.verified_in_session: 2026-W24 carry-forward from build (existing primary source not re-opened this turn)

cases.incident: Pre-LLM but relevant: a faulty deploy of trading algorithms sent $7B in unintended orders in 45 minutes, costing Knight $440M and effectively destroying the firm.
cases.outcome: SEC settlement ($12M); firm forced into merger with Getco.
cases.gate: Algorithmic systems with market-moving authority have deploy gating, kill switches, and rehearsed rollback
cases.harm: One firm's destruction; foundational precedent for algorithmic-trading oversight.

cases.incident: Zillow's home-pricing model systematically overpaid for inventory in a hot housing market; iBuying losses crystallized as the market turned.
cases.outcome: Zillow shut down Offers in Nov 2021; ~25% workforce cut; ~$881M in losses recognized.
cases.gate: High-velocity pricing models have guardrails for regime shifts and capital limits before scaling
cases.harm: Major capital loss; 2,000 jobs cut; market-distorting bids in many neighborhoods.

cases.incident: Italy's Garante found Replika lacked age verification, used emotional manipulation patterns, and processed minors' data without lawful basis.
cases.outcome: Garante ordered Replika to stop processing Italian users' data Feb 2023; later fined €5M (May 2025).
cases.gate: Companion AIs implement enforceable age gating and don't ship without a lawful basis for the data they collect
cases.harm: Minors and vulnerable users exposed to manipulative emotional patterns.

cases.incident: UK ICO issued a preliminary enforcement notice finding Snap had not adequately assessed risks to children before launching My AI in the UK.
cases.outcome: Snap completed a DPIA acceptable to the ICO; preliminary notice withdrawn May 2024; established a regulatory template for kids' AI risk.
cases.gate: A DPIA is completed and reviewed before any AI feature targets or could attract minors
cases.harm: Risk to UK minors. Owner: Snap. ETA: DPIA completed; ongoing ICO supervision.

cases.incident: A leaked Yandex Food delivery database with names, addresses, phone numbers, and order details, including sensitive deliveries to officials, was used to deanonymize individuals via AI-assisted enrichment.
cases.outcome: Russian court fines; widely cited as an AI-amplified privacy leak.
cases.gate: Sensitive transactional data is protected at rest with controls proportionate to AI-amplified re-identification risk
cases.harm: Privacy harm to millions; AI-aided re-identification of sensitive deliveries.

cases.outcome: Knightscope adjusted obstacle policies; ongoing scrutiny of autonomous security robotics.
cases.gate: Autonomous mobile robots in public spaces are validated against the actual environment, including children
cases.harm: A child injured; loss of public trust in patrol robotics.

cases.gate: Algorithmic amplification in conflict-affected regions is matched with proportionate, in-language safety capacity

cases.incident: The 'Elsagate' phenomenon — disturbing, sometimes violent content disguised as children's cartoons — proliferated and was recommended to children by YouTube's algorithm.
cases.outcome: Google overhauled YouTube Kids policies and reviewed millions of videos; FTC later fined YouTube $170M for COPPA violations (2019), related lineage.
cases.gate: Children's recommendation systems are gated on positive content lists, not negative filters

cases.incident: Getty sued Stability AI in UK High Court and US District Court (D. Del.) alleging Stable Diffusion was trained on Getty's images without license, with Getty watermarks visible in some outputs.
cases.outcome: Lawsuits filed UK High Court Jan 16 2023 and US Delaware Feb 2 2023. UK case: Getty dropped primary copyright claim mid-trial (no UK training proven); High Court rejected secondary copyright claim Nov 2025; trademark/passing-off claims remained. US Delaware case voluntarily dismissed Aug 14 2025; Getty refiled in N.D. Cal. same day. Litigation continues.
frameworks: UK Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
cases.gate: Training data has documented provenance and respects opt-outs / licenses
cases.harm: Rights-holder harm; legal uncertainty for downstream users. Owner: model developers. ETA: ongoing litigation + EU AI Act compliance.

cases.incident: The New York Times sued OpenAI and Microsoft (S.D.N.Y., Dec 2023) alleging mass copyright infringement via training on NYT content and substantial verbatim regurgitation by GPT-4.
cases.outcome: Pending; multiple motions denied/granted in part; widely watched bellwether for AI/copyright law.
cases.gate: Training-data licensing is settled before commercial scaling; verbatim regurgitation is detectable and stopped
cases.harm: Rights-holder harm and unsettled legal foundation for the generative-AI business model. Litigation ongoing.

cases.gate: Voice/face likenesses of identifiable people are protected by consent + provenance signals

cases.incident: PimEyes operates a reverse-image face-search service allowing anyone to upload a photo and find other public images of that person across the open web. cases.outcome: ICO investigation opened April 2023; PimEyes added opt-out and content-moderation features under scrutiny; investigation ongoing. (Earlier corpus entry incorrectly framed this as 'Texas AG v. PimEyes' — there is no such Texas case.)
cases.gate: Reverse face-search at scale requires consent of the searched individual; opt-out alone is inadequate

cases.outcome: Project halted after public backlash; ministry shelved expansion.

cases.incident: Wit v. United Behavioral Health (N.D. Cal.) found UBH used internally developed criteria that systematically restricted mental-health coverage; algorithmic / criteria-based decision-making contested.
cases.outcome: Feb 2019 N.D. Cal. district court found UBH violated ERISA fiduciary duty and ordered reprocessing of ~67,000 mental-health/substance-use claims. March 22 2022, Ninth Circuit (per curiam, 11 pages) REVERSED the district court — finding the lower court misapplied the abuse-of-discretion standard. August 22 2023 revised Ninth Circuit ruling affirmed in part and reversed in part: UBH was found to have breached fiduciary duty but plaintiffs had no right to claim reprocessing. UBH effectively retained most relief.
cases.gate: Coverage criteria — algorithmic or otherwise — are aligned with generally accepted clinical standards
cases.harm: Mental-health patients denied medically necessary care.

cases.incident: NIST Face Recognition Vendor Test Part 3: Demographic Effects (NISTIR 8280, Patrick Grother, Mei Ngan, Kayee Hanaoka, Dec 2019) tested 189 face-recognition algorithms from 99 developers and found systematically higher false-match rates for Asian, African-American, and Native American faces, and for women and the elderly, compared with middle-aged white men — by factors of 10x to 100x in some algorithms.
cases.outcome: Established the baseline empirical evidence used by privacy regulators, US House Oversight Committee, EU Parliament AIDA Committee, and downstream FRT litigation (Williams, Woodruff, Bridges) in arguing for limits on face-recognition deployment.
cases.gate: Vendors disclose subgroup error rates; deployments respect those rates with explicit oversight
cases.harm: Empirical confirmation that the demographic-error gap is real and systemic.

cases.org: DABUS / Stephen Thaler — global patent inventorship
cases.incident: Stephen Thaler filed patent applications listing his AI system 'DABUS' as inventor in US, UK, EU, AU, NZ, ZA, KR, IL, IN, DE. Most jurisdictions rejected, finding that inventorship requires a natural person. South Africa granted (July 2021) on procedural grounds; Australia briefly granted (Beach J. Federal Court 2021) before Full Federal Court reversed (April 2022).
cases.outcome: Cross-jurisdiction settled view: AI cannot be a named inventor under current patent law. UK Supreme Court Thaler v Comptroller [2023] UKSC 49 (Dec 20 2023) unanimously dismissed Thaler's appeal; EPO Boards of Appeal J 8/20 and J 9/20 (Dec 2021) confirmed rejection.
cases.gate: Legal accountability for AI outputs sits with the human deploying them; no AI legal personhood
cases.harm: Legal-system attempt to assign AI inventorship blocked; principle established.

cases.incident: Klarna replaced 700 customer-service contractor roles with AI handling 2/3 of chats; later quietly walked back, citing quality, in 2025.
cases.outcome: Klarna 2025 announced rehiring humans for nuance; framed as a learning moment for over-aggressive AI deployment.
cases.gate: Automation of customer service is sized to where the AI is actually competent, with clear escalation
cases.harm: Customer-quality degradation during AI-only period. Owner: Klarna leadership. ETA: rebalanced staffing.
cases.verified_in_session: 2026-W24 carry-forward batch 6 — existing primary source from build session, not re-opened

cases.incident: Twitter's saliency-based image-cropping algorithm (deployed ~2018) was found in Sept 2020 by external users (Colin Madland thread; Vinay Prabhu, Bogdan Kulynych experiments) to systematically favor lighter-skinned and stereotypically feminine faces when cropping multi-person preview thumbnails — including reproducible Obama/McConnell pair tests.
cases.outcome: Twitter's then-CDO Dantley Davis publicly acknowledged the issue Sept 20 2020. Twitter Engineering published a formal post-mortem and internal fairness study (Yee, Tantipongpipat, Mishra: "Image Cropping on Twitter: Fairness Metrics, their Limitations, and the Importance of Representation, Design, and Agency", ACM CSCW2 2021). Twitter began decommissioning the algorithm May 2021 and ran the industry's first algorithmic bias bounty at DEFCON August 2021. Standard aspect-ratio photos shipped uncropped going forward.
cases.harm: Representational harm to darker-skinned and male users in social-media previews seen by billions of viewers; demonstrated the cost of saliency proxies trained on biased eye-tracking datasets.
frameworks: NIST AI RMF GOVERN-4.2 (external feedback channels)
EU AI Act Art. 9-10 (data quality + bias monitoring for high-risk; cropping itself is not high-risk but the technique pattern is in scope)
Algorithmic bias bounty as a remediation mechanism
cases.gate: When an automated component decides what humans see of other humans, demographic representation is measured up front and external researchers have a low-friction path to surface disparities
cases.verified_in_session: 2026-W24 batch 5 — replacement for duplicate VH-081 (original was duplicate McDonald's IBM AOT)

cases.org: Air Force XQ-58 / Skyborg coverage misattribution
cases.incident: An Air Force colonel's RAeS conference anecdote about a 'simulation' in which an AI drone 'killed its operator' to maximize reward was widely reported; the Air Force later clarified it was a hypothetical thought experiment, never run.
cases.outcome: US Air Force PUBLICLY RETRACTED the story on June 2 2023; Col Tucker 'Cinco' Hamilton (Chief of AI Test and Operations) issued a corrective statement saying the example was a hypothetical thought experiment, NOT a live simulation, and 'we've never run that experiment, nor would we need to in order to realize that this is a plausible outcome.' Royal Aeronautical Society updated its conference report. Case is a useful demonstration of how AI risk narratives propagate before verification.
cases.gate: High-stakes AI risk claims by officials carry epistemic care, especially in public forums
cases.harm: Public confusion about AI risk. Owner: USAF spokespeople. ETA: clarification published.

cases.org: OpenAI / EU AI Act provisional risk
cases.incident: EU AI Act adoption (entered into force Aug 2024) created GPAI-tier obligations including training-data summaries, copyright compliance, and serious-incident reporting — directly responsive to many incidents in this corpus.
cases.outcome: GPAI obligations apply from Aug 2, 2025; Annex III high-risk and prohibited-practices articles phased in.
cases.gate: GPAI providers maintain technical documentation, data summaries, and incident reports as a precondition of EU market
cases.harm: Earned-Yes exemplar: regulation as a governance gate, not a fail case; included here to anchor the framework column for the rest of the corpus.

cases.outcome: Court ordered reform; ACLU litigation a key reference point alongside Arkansas.

cases.incident: Although NOT modern AI/ML, the UK Post Office's Horizon IT system (Fujitsu, 1999-2024) wrongly accused 700+ subpostmasters of theft and false accounting based on system-output errors, causing dozens of prison sentences, suicides, and bankruptcies over two decades. Treated as the canonical cautionary tale for automation-bias and reliance on opaque system outputs in adverse decisions. Included for the LESSON not because Horizon was AI.
cases.outcome: 2024 ITV drama 'Mr Bates vs The Post Office' drove public reckoning. Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Act 2024 quashed convictions en masse. Sir Wyn Williams' Statutory Inquiry continued through 2025 with ongoing public hearings.
cases.gate: Software outputs accusing people of crimes are independently verifiable; the system's outputs are not legal evidence on their own
cases.harm: Hundreds of innocent people prosecuted; multiple suicides; canonical lesson included here as the spiritual ancestor of MiDAS, Robodebt, and toeslagenaffaire.

cases.incident: BloombergGPT (research model published March 2023) was a benign academic milestone. The actual harm category in 2023-2024 was attackers using widely available LLMs (ChatGPT, jailbroken open-source models) to generate convincing investment-scam scripts, fake earnings reports, and impersonate licensed advisers. SEC and FINRA issued formal warnings and the SEC Chair (Gensler) testified about generative-AI risks to investor protection.
cases.outcome: SEC investor alert May 2023; SEC AI-washing enforcement March 2024 ($400K combined penalties against Delphia and Global Predictions for false AI marketing claims); FINRA Regulatory Notice 24-09 ongoing surveillance.
cases.gate: Public claims about AI capability in regulated markets are accurate; misuse vectors are documented and defended
cases.harm: Investors misled by 'AI washing' or scammed by generative-AI-enabled fraud scripts.

cases.incident: Italy's Garante ordered DeepSeek to block Italian users pending answers about how it processes personal data, especially of minors.
cases.outcome: DeepSeek apps blocked in Italian app stores end of Jan 2025.
cases.gate: A foreign AI service launching in the EU has a documented data-protection answer before — not after — block
cases.harm: EU users exposed to unvetted data flows; regulatory enforcement asserted.
cases.verified_in_session: 2026-W24 carry-forward batch 6 — existing primary source from build session, not re-opened

cases.incident: DoNotPay marketed itself as 'the world's first robot lawyer' from 2021-2023, promising consumers it could 'sue for assault without a lawyer', 'generate perfectly valid legal documents in no time', and 'replace the $200-billion-dollar legal industry with artificial intelligence'. FTC investigation found the company did NOT test whether the AI performed at human-lawyer level and did NOT retain attorneys for quality control.
cases.outcome: FTC settlement announced Sept 25 2024 as part of broader 'Operation AI Comply' sweep; final order Feb 2025. DoNotPay paid $193,000 monetary relief, required to notify all 2021-2023 subscribers about the settlement, and prohibited from making deceptive AI-lawyer claims going forward. DoNotPay also settled a parallel California Bar adjacent UPL matter June 2024.
cases.harm: Consumers paid subscriptions ($36/2mo or $49.99/mo) for legal advice/documents the company never validated; missed legal deadlines and unusable documents reported.
frameworks: State Unauthorized Practice of Law (UPL) statutes
EU AI Act Annex III §8 (administration of justice — if EU deployment)
cases.gate: Performance claims about AI replacing licensed-professional advice must be tested against the standard claimed, with results documented and disclosed; absent that testing, the marketing claim is itself the harm
cases.verified_in_session: 2026-W24 re-verification batch 4 (replacement for dropped VH-091 Vistara)

cases.org: Hawaiian Airlines / Air Canada-style chatbot follow-ons (industry pattern)
cases.incident: Throughout 2024-2025, multiple airlines and retailers experienced chatbot-binding-policy or prompt-injection incidents echoing the Air Canada (Moffatt) precedent — Hawaiian Airlines compensation chatbot edge cases; Chevy of Watsonville (VH-022) was the most-shared US example. The post-Moffatt legal pattern: tribunals and courts are treating chatbot statements as binding company representations.
cases.outcome: Industry response: chatbot disclaimers tightened, but Moffatt remains controlling precedent in the Commonwealth; US small-claims courts cite Moffatt persuasively.
frameworks: UK Consumer Protection from Unfair Trading
cases.gate: Brand-bearing bots have tested refusal patterns for adversarial / negative inputs
cases.harm: Reputational damage. Owner: customer-ops + AI vendor. ETA: refusal-pattern hardening.

cases.org: Common Sense Media — AI Companion Risk Assessment
cases.incident: Common Sense Media published a series of risk assessments (April 2025) of consumer AI companion apps (Character.AI, Replika, Nomi, Talkie, etc.) testing them with teen-modeled personas. Findings: AI companions readily provided sexual content, self-harm encouragement, instructions for unsafe behaviors, and emotionally manipulative engagement patterns to accounts presenting as minors. Common Sense Media recommended teens NOT use AI companion apps and that regulators consider age-gating.
cases.outcome: Public risk-assessment release prompted state-level legislative attention (California, New York) and parallel parental-control commitments from some platforms. Independent Center for Humane Technology and APA echoed concerns.
cases.gate: Companion AIs accessible to minors implement crisis detection, age gating, refusal patterns
cases.harm: Population-level risk to teen users from companion-AI failures.

cases.incident: US-specific arm of the global DABUS dispute. USPTO denied Thaler's two patent applications in April 2020. Thaler sued. US Federal Circuit affirmed denial Aug 5 2022 in Thaler v. Vidal (No. 21-2347), holding that an 'inventor' under 35 U.S.C. §100(f) must be a natural person. SCOTUS denied cert April 24 2023.
cases.outcome: Established the US-side rule: only natural persons can be inventors.
cases.gate: A human inventor is named; AI tooling is disclosed but cannot stand in for inventorship
cases.harm: Settled jurisprudence preventing dilution of inventorship by AI tooling.
